questions.question: What are the two main types of carbon-based fuels?

Which coal type is found deepest underground?
What are hydrocarbons?
options: Compounds of only carbon and oxygen
Compounds of carbon and hydrogen bonded to each other
Compounds of carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen
questions.correct: Compounds of carbon and hydrogen bonded to each other

questions.question: Unbranched alkanes are gases up to which carbon number?
Unbranched alkanes are liquids in what carbon range?
What is the primary component of natural gas?
What percentage of natural gas is methane?
What is combustion?
options: Slow absorption of oxygen by a fuel
Rapid combination of fuel with oxygen releasing heat and light
The separation of hydrocarbons by boiling point
The storage of chemical energy in a molecule
questions.correct: Rapid combination of fuel with oxygen releasing heat and light

questions.question: What are the most common products of combustion?
What is the balanced equation for methane combustion?

In an endothermic reaction, energy change is:

What happens to heat of combustion as hydrogen-to-carbon ratio decreases?
What happens to heat of combustion as oxygen content in a fuel increases?
What is the heat of combustion of methane?
What is the heat of combustion of octane?
What is the heat of combustion of wood (glucose)?
What is fracking used to extract?
options: Oil and natural gas from hard shale rock formations
questions.correct: Oil and natural gas from hard shale rock formations

questions.question: How deep does fracking drill into the Earth?

options: Fracking fluid (water, sand, and chemicals) under pressure
questions.correct: Fracking fluid (water, sand, and chemicals) under pressure

questions.question: What process is used to separate crude oil into its components?
What carbon chain length range makes up petrol (gasoline)?
What carbon chain length range makes up diesel?
What is thermal cracking?
options: Using a catalyst to split molecules at low temperature
Breaking large molecules by heating them to high temperature
questions.correct: Breaking large molecules by heating them to high temperature

questions.question: What is catalytic cracking?
options: Breaking molecules by heating to very high temperatures
Using a catalyst to lower the temperature required for splitting molecules
Rearranging atoms within a molecule to form isomers
questions.correct: Using a catalyst to lower the temperature required for splitting molecules

questions.question: What is catalytic reforming?
options: Separating crude oil by boiling point
Breaking large molecules into smaller ones with heat
Rearranging atoms within a molecule to produce branched isomers
questions.correct: Rearranging atoms within a molecule to produce branched isomers

questions.question: Why is iso-octane preferred over n-octane in gasoline engines?
options: It has a higher boiling point
It contains more energy per gram

questions.question: What does a higher octane rating indicate?
options: Fuel ignites easily under low compression
Fuel can withstand more compression before igniting
Fuel has a lower boiling point
questions.correct: Fuel can withstand more compression before igniting

questions.question: What was tetraethyl lead (TEL) used for in gasoline?
What are the two main gasoline additives used after TEL was eliminated?
How much energy does ethanol release per mole?
How much energy does octane release per mole?
What percentage of ethanol is typically blended into gasoline?
Why can't automobile engines run on pure ethanol?
options: It contains less energy per amount burned than gasoline hydrocarbons
It does not combust at all
questions.correct: It contains less energy per amount burned than gasoline hydrocarbons

questions.question: What does breaking bonds in a chemical reaction require?
What does forming bonds in a chemical reaction do?
Where is oil found underground?
options: In liquid pools beneath impermeable rock
Within pores of geologic rock formations like sandstone
In volcanic rock near the surface
questions.correct: Within pores of geologic rock formations like sandstone

questions.question: What is the full name of MTBE, a gasoline additive?
What happens to energy during each transformation in power generation?
options: Energy is fully conserved with no loss
Energy is wasted during each transformation
questions.correct: Energy is wasted during each transformation

questions.question: What are the two principal environmental impacts of CO2?
Unlike fossil fuels, how do biofuels interact with CO2?
options: They produce more CO2 than fossil fuels
They do not produce any CO2 at all
They actively participate in the carbon cycle through photosynthesis
questions.correct: They actively participate in the carbon cycle through photosynthesis

questions.question: What are the three main sources of CO2 emissions listed in the slides?
options: Power stations, vehicle use, and heating
questions.correct: Power stations, vehicle use, and heating

questions.question: What does carbon monoxide (CO) combine with in the blood?
What is carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)?
options: Hemoglobin combined with CO, ineffective at delivering oxygen to tissues
A protein that neutralizes carbon monoxide
A form of oxygen found in red blood cells
questions.correct: Hemoglobin combined with CO, ineffective at delivering oxygen to tissues

questions.question: What is the medical term for CO poisoning caused by conversion of Hb to COHb?
What property do greenhouse gases share?
options: They reflect all sunlight away from Earth
They dissolve ozone in the stratosphere
They produce acid rain when they combine with water

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a greenhouse gas in the slides?
What causes global warming according to the slides?
options: Increased UV radiation from the sun
Increased concentration of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere
Depletion of the ozone layer releasing heat
questions.correct: Increased concentration of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere

questions.question: What are the health consequences of ozone layer depletion?
options: Increased skin cancer, eye damage, and harm to biological species
questions.correct: Increased skin cancer, eye damage, and harm to biological species

questions.question: In what layer of the atmosphere does ozone depletion occur?
What does CFCs stand for?
Which of the following is a property of CFCs?
options: High boiling point and highly flammable
Low boiling point, chemically inert, nontoxic, nonflammable, insoluble in water
questions.correct: Low boiling point, chemically inert, nontoxic, nonflammable, insoluble in water

questions.question: Why are CFCs used as refrigerants and aerosol propellants?
options: Because they are cheap and easy to produce
Because of their useful properties: low boiling point, inert, nontoxic, nonflammable
Because they are soluble in water
questions.correct: Because of their useful properties: low boiling point, inert, nontoxic, nonflammable

questions.question: How does the chlorine radical from CFCs destroy ozone?
options: It reacts with ozone to form chlorine gas and water
It breaks down the ozone molecule and the resulting ClO• radical destroys more ozone in a cycle
It combines with ozone to form a stable compound
It reflects UV radiation preventing ozone formation
questions.correct: It breaks down the ozone molecule and the resulting ClO• radical destroys more ozone in a cycle

questions.question: How many ozone molecules can be destroyed by breaking a single C-Cl bond?
What is the overall reaction when 2 ozone molecules are destroyed by CFCs?
How does NO2 contribute to ozone layer destruction?
options: It directly absorbs UV radiation destroying ozone
NO2 reacts with ozone producing NO•, which reacts with more ozone — catalyzing destruction and regenerating NO2
It combines with ozone to form nitric acid
It releases chlorine radicals that attack ozone
questions.correct: NO2 reacts with ozone producing NO•, which reacts with more ozone — catalyzing destruction and regenerating NO2

questions.question: What is the overall equation for NO2 reacting with ozone?
Which gas, similar to NO2, can also break down the ozone layer?
What is acid rain?
options: Rain that contains dissolved CO2 only
The return to the terrestrial/aquatic environment of carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur oxides in acidic form
Rain caused by high temperatures evaporating industrial chemicals
Rain with a pH higher than 7
questions.correct: The return to the terrestrial/aquatic environment of carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur oxides in acidic form

questions.question: Which fossil fuel contains particularly large quantities of sulfur?
What acids are found in acid rain?
What effect does acid precipitation have on rivers and lakes?
options: It raises the pH making them more alkaline
The drop in pH has significant adverse effects on aquatic ecosystems
It increases the oxygen content of water
questions.correct: The drop in pH has significant adverse effects on aquatic ecosystems

questions.question: What was the pH of the storm in Scotland in 1974?
The rain in Kane, Pennsylvania on September 19, 1978 had a pH lower than what common substance?
What health effects can lead exposure cause?
options: Nervous system damage, digestive problems, cancer, mental retardation, and brain disorders
questions.correct: Nervous system damage, digestive problems, cancer, mental retardation, and brain disorders

questions.question: What is tetraethyl lead (TEL) used for in gasoline?
options: To prevent engine knocking and engine damage
questions.correct: To prevent engine knocking and engine damage

questions.question: What pollutants does TEL release when it burns with fuel?
options: Lead oxides (PbO, Pb2O) and atomic Pb
questions.correct: Lead oxides (PbO, Pb2O) and atomic Pb

questions.question: Where is lead commonly found besides gasoline?
options: In diesel, lead batteries, paints, and hair dye products
questions.correct: In diesel, lead batteries, paints, and hair dye products

questions.question: What is thermal pollution in the context of power generation?
options: Heat released from burning coal warming the air directly
Water used to collect waste heat during power generation, increasing atmospheric temperature
CO2 trapping extra heat from the sun
Ozone depletion allowing more UV radiation to heat the Earth
questions.correct: Water used to collect waste heat during power generation, increasing atmospheric temperature

questions.question: Which of the following is a listed solution to environmental problems from energy production?
options: Clean renewable energy technologies and encouraging forestation
Reducing public awareness of energy issues
questions.correct: Clean renewable energy technologies and encouraging forestation

questions.question: What does CCS stand for?
What is the goal of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technology?
options: To reduce carbon emissions by capturing CO2 at the source
To convert CO2 into oxygen using solar energy
To store fossil fuels underground for future use
questions.correct: To reduce carbon emissions by capturing CO2 at the source

questions.question: What is Direct Air Capture (DAC)?
options: A method of capturing CO2 directly from factory smokestacks
A technology that removes CO2 directly from atmospheric air
A system for filtering particulates from engine exhaust
A process for converting CO2 into biofuel underground
questions.correct: A technology that removes CO2 directly from atmospheric air

questions.question: Why is DAC considered less viable than post-combustion CCS currently?
options: DAC has been widely accepted as too expensive to implement compared to source capture
DAC only works in cold climates
questions.correct: DAC has been widely accepted as too expensive to implement compared to source capture

questions.question: What was the APS 2011 'realistic' cost estimate for DAC?
What did Carbon Engineering's 2018 pilot data forecast for DAC costs?
When was Carbon Engineering founded and what is its mission?
options: 2009, to develop and commercialize technology that captures industrial-scale CO2 from air
2015, to build wind farms in Canada
questions.correct: 2009, to develop and commercialize technology that captures industrial-scale CO2 from air

questions.question: What can the CO2 captured by Carbon Engineering be used for?
options: Stored underground or synthesized into clean transportation fuels
Released back into the atmosphere after purification
Converted into oxygen for medical use
questions.correct: Stored underground or synthesized into clean transportation fuels

questions.question: What has atmospheric CO2 risen to due to reliance on climate self-regulation?
Which of the following can result in depletion of the ozone layer?
options: Oxides of nitrogen and sulfur only
CFCs, oxides of nitrogen and sulfur — all of the above
questions.correct: CFCs, oxides of nitrogen and sulfur — all of the above

questions.question: What is the main concern related to CO2 emissions?
options: Global warming, enhanced greenhouse effect, and ocean acidification
questions.correct: Global warming, enhanced greenhouse effect, and ocean acidification

questions.question: Which statement about carbon monoxide (CO) is correct?
options: It is less reactive than oxygen so it rarely binds to hemoglobin
It only affects the lungs and not the blood
It combines with hemoglobin making it ineffective for delivering oxygen to bodily tissues
It combines with plasma proteins but not hemoglobin
questions.correct: It combines with hemoglobin making it ineffective for delivering oxygen to bodily tissues

questions.question: Why is energy storage important for electricity systems?
options: Electricity demand fluctuates and supply must match demand
Energy is stored only for emergencies
questions.correct: Electricity demand fluctuates and supply must match demand

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as an intermittent/alternative energy source in the slides?

options: Energy is stored when supply exceeds demand and released when demand exceeds supply
Energy is always produced at a constant rate
Storage is only used for fossil fuels
questions.correct: Energy is stored when supply exceeds demand and released when demand exceeds supply

The net energy released by the reaction
questions.correct: The energy barrier that must be overcome to start the reaction

questions.correct: Water is pumped into the pit, storing potential energy

questions.correct: To store coolness for later use in refrigeration or air-conditioning

Combine with oxygen to form Li₂O

options: Voltage × number of movable Li ions / total battery weight or volume
questions.correct: Voltage × number of movable Li ions / total battery weight or volume

options: It can easily be liquefied at room temperature without pressurization

questions.correct: Natural gas extracted from rock formations

As nuclear energy in plant nuclei

options: The same amount they emit when rotting organically
questions.correct: The same amount they emit when rotting organically

Methane from anaerobic decomposition of waste

options: Corn, sugar cane, and sweet sorghum

questions.question: What is 'gasohol'?

options: It increases km per litre significantly
It has no effect on performance

questions.correct: It reduces the specific energy; a car on E15 gets ~90% km/L vs pure gasoline

questions.question: What percentage of denatured ethanol does E85 contain?

questions.correct: Oily seeds such as cotton seeds, palm oil, olive oil residue, and pumpkin seeds

questions.question: Do standard diesel engines need modification to run on biodiesel?
options: Yes, only the fuel injectors need replacing
No, standard diesel engines do not need modification
Yes, a new fuel pump is always required
questions.correct: No, standard diesel engines do not need modification

questions.question: Which of the following is a disadvantage of biomass related to food supply?
options: Crops like corn and sugar cane used for energy compete with food — the 'to eat or to burn' dilemma
questions.correct: Crops like corn and sugar cane used for energy compete with food — the 'to eat or to burn' dilemma

questions.question: What is a key environmental effect of growing biomass for energy related to water?
options: Biomass crops reduce overall irrigation needs
High requirements for fresh water for irrigation and processing
Biomass increases rainfall in dry regions
questions.correct: High requirements for fresh water for irrigation and processing

questions.question: Anaerobic decomposition of biomass always unintentionally produces which greenhouse gas?
options: Methane, which contributes to global warming
questions.correct: Methane, which contributes to global warming

questions.question: According to the future outlook of biomass, what is considered a GOOD use?
options: Using food crops like corn for large-scale fuel production
Converting vast tracts of farmland into energy crop fields
Using agricultural and forestry wastes in co-firing and extracting biofuels from waste
questions.correct: Using agricultural and forestry wastes in co-firing and extracting biofuels from waste

questions.question: When was the Atikokan Generating Station converted to biomass operation?
What is the power capacity of the Atikokan Generating Station?
What fuel does the Thunder Bay Generating Station use after its conversion?
What is the largest single source of renewable energy in Canada by percentage?
What percentage of Canada's total renewable energy comes from solid biomass (wood/waste)?
What is the conversion efficiency disadvantage of biomass compared to photovoltaic solar cells?
options: Biomass is more efficient than solar PV
Biomass conversion efficiency (0.1–1.5%) is significantly lower than solar PV
Biomass is only slightly less efficient
questions.correct: Biomass conversion efficiency (0.1–1.5%) is significantly lower than solar PV

questions.question: In the future outlook, what is expected to INCREASE for biomass production?
options: Energy crop production on large farmlands

questions.question: Why is nuclear fuel considered significantly more efficient than fossil fuels?
options: It is about 8,000 times more efficient than fossil fuels
questions.correct: It is about 8,000 times more efficient than fossil fuels

questions.question: What is radioactivity?
options: The emission of light from heated metals
The spontaneous transformation of an unstable nucleus into another element
The absorption of neutrons by a stable nucleus
The splitting of a heavy nucleus by a neutron
questions.correct: The spontaneous transformation of an unstable nucleus into another element

questions.question: What does 'half-life' (t₁/₂) mean?
options: The time for a sample to completely decay
The time for half the atoms to fission
The time for radioactivity to fall to one-half its initial value
The time between two successive chain reactions
questions.correct: The time for radioactivity to fall to one-half its initial value

questions.question: How long is the half-life of plutonium-239?
Which radioisotope has the shortest half-life listed in the slides?
What radiation dose (in Sievert) is likely to cause death?
Who first demonstrated nuclear fission in 1938?
In the fission of U-235, approximately how many free neutrons are released?
What is the critical mass of U-235 needed for spontaneous fission?
Using E = mc², how much energy (in joules) is released by fissioning 1.0 kg of U-235 (0.1% mass loss)?
How does a nuclear power plant differ from a conventional power plant?
options: It uses turbines instead of generators
The reactor replaces the boiler/combustion chamber
It produces DC current instead of AC
It does not use steam at all
questions.correct: The reactor replaces the boiler/combustion chamber

questions.question: What are the four main components of a nuclear reactor?
options: Fuel, moderator/coolant, control rods, safety devices
Fission chamber, turbine, condenser, cooling tower
questions.correct: Fuel, moderator/coolant, control rods, safety devices

questions.question: What chemical form is uranium fuel typically used in inside a reactor?
Which uranium isotope is the only naturally occurring fissile material?
What percentage of U-235 does slightly enriched reactor fuel typically contain?
Which element is commonly used to make control rods because it absorbs neutrons?
What reaction occurs in a boron control rod when it absorbs a neutron?
What is the key difference between the CANDU reactor and a standard pressurized water reactor (PWR)?
options: CANDU uses graphite as a moderator
CANDU uses heavy water (D₂O) as coolant and moderator with natural uranium fuel
CANDU operates at much higher temperatures
CANDU burns plutonium instead of uranium
questions.correct: CANDU uses heavy water (D₂O) as coolant and moderator with natural uranium fuel

questions.question: Why does a light-water (H₂O) reactor require enriched uranium while the CANDU does not?
options: H₂O produces more neutrons per fission
H₂O has a higher capture cross-section for neutrons, absorbing more of them
D₂O is cheaper to produce than H₂O
questions.correct: H₂O has a higher capture cross-section for neutrons, absorbing more of them

questions.question: What caused the Three Mile Island (TMI-2) accident in 1979?
options: An earthquake destroyed the coolant pipes
Failure of a condensate pump and a non-operational emergency cooling system
A hydrogen gas explosion blew off the reactor shield
questions.correct: Failure of a condensate pump and a non-operational emergency cooling system

questions.question: What was the maximum temperature reached inside TMI-2 during the accident?
What made the Chernobyl accident more catastrophic than TMI?
options: Chernobyl used enriched uranium while TMI used natural uranium
The graphite control rods caught fire and the reactor shield was destroyed, releasing radioactive material
Chernobyl had no containment building at all
The reactor exploded because of a fission bomb design
questions.correct: The graphite control rods caught fire and the reactor shield was destroyed, releasing radioactive material

questions.question: What triggered the Fukushima nuclear disaster?
options: Operator error during a safety test
A pipeline failure causing coolant loss
An earthquake followed by a tsunami that knocked out electrical generators for cooling
A hydrogen leak ignited near the fuel rods
questions.correct: An earthquake followed by a tsunami that knocked out electrical generators for cooling

questions.question: All three major nuclear accidents shared which common cause?
options: Loss of coolant to the reactor core
Failure of the control rod system
questions.correct: Loss of coolant to the reactor core

questions.question: What is the purpose of a fast breeder reactor (FBR)?
options: To produce electricity more cheaply than conventional reactors
To convert fertile U-238 into fissile Pu-239 while also producing heat/steam
To run on thorium instead of uranium
To cool the reactor with liquid metal instead of water
questions.correct: To convert fertile U-238 into fissile Pu-239 while also producing heat/steam

questions.question: For how many years could North American high-grade uranium reserves supply energy if only U-235 is used?

options: Splitting of a heavy nucleus into two smaller nuclei
Combining light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus, releasing energy
Decay of an unstable isotope by emitting radiation
Converting fertile isotopes into fissile isotopes
questions.correct: Combining light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus, releasing energy

They only work in cold climates
